q: A capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of an electric field.
The charging process is viewed as the apparent transfer of charge between two conductors.
Work is done to transfer charge from a conductor at higher potential to one at lower potential.
The energy stored by a capacitor is equal to the area under the curve of a $Q-V$ graph.
The work done in charging a capacitor is equal to $QV$.
Before a capacitor is charged, the potential difference between its two conductors is zero.
The average potential difference of the capacitor during the charging process is $\frac{V}{2}$.
The energy of a capacitor can be expressed as $W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
Placing a resistor in the charging circuit speeds up the charging process.
The greater the values of resistance and capacitance, the longer it takes to charge the capacitor.
The product of the resistance $R$ and the capacitance $C$ is called the time constant $\tau$.
The time constant $\tau$ is measured in seconds.
During the charging process, the capacitor voltage falls off with time.
In the discharging process, the voltage of the capacitor falls off with time.
A capacitor is discharged through a load resistor by opening switch $S_1$ and closing switch $S_2$.
